projects.title: Robot Hand Real-Time Teleoperation Interface
projects.shortSubtitle: Real-Time Teleoperation via Computer Vision and URDF Control
projects.summary: A browser-based teleoperation interface that maps 21 hand keypoints from MediaPipe to joint angles on 31 URDF robotic hand models in real time. The system implements a full perception-to-actuation pipeline: webcam CV, hand-to-joint kinematic mapping, and 3D visualization of articulated robot models via Three.js.
projects.content:
Browser-based Hand Tracking for Robotic Hand Control

Overview

A real-time 3D visualization platform that uses webcam-based hand tracking to control robotic hand models entirely in the browser. Built with MediaPipe and Three.js, it tracks 21 hand keypoints via AI and maps them to 31 different robotic hand models with zero installation required.

Focus

  • Working across frontend, computer vision, 3d graphics so the concept and execution stayed aligned.
  • Designing the interaction around short sessions, clear feedback, and the performance limits of camera-based or mobile AR experiences.
  • Balancing technical experimentation with an interaction model that still reads immediately to first-time users.
  • Building with React 18, Zustand, Three.js, React Three Fiber to match the needs of the project without overcomplicating the stack.

Approach

A real-time 3D visualization platform that uses webcam-based hand tracking to control robotic hand models entirely in the browser. Built with MediaPipe and Three.js, it tracks 21 hand keypoints via AI and maps them to 31 different robotic hand models with zero installation required. The interesting part of the work was making the interaction feel immediate while still respecting device constraints, camera input, and the expectation that AR experiences should teach themselves quickly. The implementation side centered on React 18, Zustand, Three.js, React Three Fiber, React Three Drei, chosen for fit rather than novelty.

The project is paired with 2 external references in the sidebar for people who want to see the live build, repository, or related documentation.

Tools and Stack

  • React 18
  • Zustand
  • Three.js
  • React Three Fiber
  • React Three Drei
  • URDF
  • MediaPipe Hands
  • Radix UI

Outcome

Built in 2025, the project reached a live state in the portfolio. It stands out as a practical AR prototype or campaign piece that combines technical problem solving with quick-read interaction design. The project is also documented through Live Simulation and YouTube Demo, which complement the material shown on this page.

projects.title: CuraLoop: AI Companion for Alzheimer's Care
projects.shortSubtitle: Real-Time AI Pipeline with Human-in-the-Loop Monitoring
projects.summary: A sensor-to-insight application that continuously processes behavioral and cognitive signals through an AI pipeline, then surfaces trends to human reviewers. Built with a Human-in-the-Loop architecture analogous to robotic monitoring systems: data ingestion, AI inference, and human oversight in a closed feedback loop.
projects.content:
1-day Humans & AI Hackathon project in Seattle

Overview

Through AI Chat + Game, the app monitors cognitive and game behavior to analyze and monitor the level of Alzheimer's care needed for anyone.

Focus

  • Working across full stack, ai integration, react native so the concept and execution stayed aligned.
  • Shaping the experience as a product, not just a demo, by thinking through information flow, usability, and implementation tradeoffs.
  • Keeping the build grounded in a concrete workflow so the interface and backend decisions reinforce each other.
  • Building with React Native, TypeScript, Expo, FastAPI to match the needs of the project without overcomplicating the stack.

Approach

Through AI Chat + Game, the app monitors cognitive and game behavior to analyze and monitor the level of Alzheimer's care needed for anyone. The challenge was less about any single screen and more about getting the full loop to make sense: how a user arrives, understands the system, completes a task, and sees the value of the tool. The implementation side centered on React Native, TypeScript, Expo, FastAPI, Python, chosen for fit rather than novelty.

The project is paired with 2 external references in the sidebar for people who want to see the live build, repository, or related documentation.

Tools and Stack

  • TypeScript
  • Expo
  • FastAPI
  • Python
  • Claude Sonnet
  • Trend Analysis

It shows a product-minded way of building: connecting interface choices, technical architecture, and the actual use case being served. projects.title: B612 Soccer
projects.shortSubtitle: Minimalist 2-player soccer on a looping planet — solo-built in 48 hours for GMTK 2025 (Top 2%)
projects.summary: B612 Soccer is a minimalist, fast-paced 2-player soccer game set on a looping circular planet. Every shot can orbit the globe for wild, unpredictable goals. Created solo for GMTK Game Jam 2025 ("Loop" theme), it features creative orbital mechanics, hand-drawn sketch visuals, and chaotic multiplayer action.
projects.content:
Development Challenge

As a 1-person team, I had only 2 days out of the 4-day jam to design, build, illustrate, and submit the entire project. Everything you see was made in that thrilling, exhausting sprint.

GMTK 2025 Jam Results

CriteriaRankScore
Enjoyment#1584.195
Creativity#2384.341
Artwork#2194.439
Audio#1224.146
Narrative#3923.756

About the Theme: "Loop"

  • Literal Loop: The ball and players both orbit the planet for truly loopy gameplay.
  • Perspective: Side view, all mechanics in polar physics.
  • Visuals: Black/white line art, blue space ambiance.
  • Pick-Up Play: 100-second matches, first to 10 goals, perfect for friends.

Core Mechanics

  • 360° Movement: Players run, jump, and kick around the full planet.
  • Kicking: Directional kicks add momentum — maybe even scoring on your own goal!
  • Physics: Natural ball orbiting, bounce, and wrapping.
  • Goals: Clear crossbars, animated feedback.
  • Multi-ball: Up to 6 balls for bonus chaos.

Controls

Player 1WASD
Player 2Arrow Keys
PauseESC

Art, Audio & Asset Production

  • Hand-drawn assets: All sprites, UI, and backgrounds sketched by pencil, scanned with a printer, and processed in Photoshop.
  • Minimalist UI: Clean, intuitive scoreboard and feedback.
  • FX: Impact effects, sparkles, blue overlays.
  • Audio: Every SFX and music track manually integrated.

My Solo Process (48 hours)

  • Day 1: Built planet, core movement, and UI framework.
  • Day 2: Implemented all scoring, game flow, multi-ball, and polish. Practically no sleep!

Implemented Features

  • Full 360° movement and orbiting gameplay
  • Score system, restart game loop
  • Multi-ball chaos (up to 6 balls)
  • Animated, hand-drawn UI and visual feedback
  • All design, code, illustration, and polish by Lingyi Zhou

Credits

Everything (Design, Code, Art, Submission): Lingyi Zhou

Special Thanks: Skinnytigerr (feedback & playtesting)

Reflection

Making B612 Soccer in just two days, solo, was an exhilarating ride — every design, art, and coding challenge hit all at once. The end result is a chaotic, replayable multiplayer game that I'm proud to share.

mediaGallery.alt: Main menu and in-game art showcase
Pencil line visual style and blue ambient backgrounds
Single- and multi-ball chaos in action
UI elements, scoreboard, and particle FX

projects.title: Just Another Day
projects.shortSubtitle: Narrative-driven game with emotional arc
projects.summary: A Unity narrative-driven game project composed of five mini-games tied together through an emotional storyline. Designed to create an emotional arc that ends in catharsis.
projects.content:
Narrative-driven game with emotional arc

Overview

A Unity narrative-driven game project composed of five mini-games tied together through an emotional storyline. Designed to create an emotional arc that ends in catharsis.

RoleGame Developer / Storytelling
StackUnity / C# / Blender

Focus

  • Working across game developer, storytelling so the concept and execution stayed aligned.
  • Translating visual ideas into repeatable assets that could hold up across multiple compositions, scenes, or use cases.
  • Iterating on form, proportion, and presentation so the final output feels intentional rather than purely technical.
  • Building with Unity, C#, Blender to match the needs of the project without overcomplicating the stack.

Approach

A Unity narrative-driven game project composed of five mini-games tied together through an emotional storyline. Designed to create an emotional arc that ends in catharsis. A large part of the process was visual iteration: testing composition, material treatment, and silhouette until the output felt coherent from multiple viewpoints instead of only in a single hero shot. The implementation side centered on Unity, C#, Blender, chosen for fit rather than novelty.

The project is paired with 2 external references in the sidebar for people who want to see the live build, repository, or related documentation.

Tools and Stack

  • Unity
  • C#
  • Blender

Outcome

This project the project reached a completed state in the portfolio. It demonstrates how visual craft, iteration, and presentation can carry just as much weight as the underlying tools. The project is also documented with references to see the live build or source.

projects.title: Ping Pong Game
projects.shortSubtitle: 2D game with 3D illusion built from scratch
projects.summary: A complete 2D game that gives 3D illusion. An interactive automate enemy that you play against. Game Engine built from scratch using p5js to manage game asset, sound, levels and game controls.
projects.content:
2D game with 3D illusion built from scratch

Overview

A complete 2D game that gives 3D illusion. An interactive automate enemy that you play against. Game Engine built from scratch using p5js to manage game asset, sound, levels and game controls.

Focus

  • Working across game engine dev so the concept and execution stayed aligned.
  • Exploring visual systems through iteration, parameter tuning, and small technical experiments rather than one fixed output.
  • Using code as a medium for motion, color, and procedural variation while keeping the work understandable to viewers.
  • Building with P5JS, JavaScript to match the needs of the project without overcomplicating the stack.

Approach

A complete 2D game that gives 3D illusion. An interactive automate enemy that you play against. Game Engine built from scratch using p5js to manage game asset, sound, levels and game controls. The work evolved through experimentation, with each sketch or shader acting as a small probe into motion, pattern, and rendering behavior rather than a one-off aesthetic exercise. The implementation side centered on P5JS, JavaScript, chosen for fit rather than novelty.

The project is paired with an external reference in the sidebar for people who want to see the live build, repository, or related documentation.

Tools and Stack

  • P5JS
  • JavaScript

Outcome

Built in 2021, the project reached a completed state in the portfolio. It works as a snapshot of ongoing experimentation and a way to show taste, graphics fundamentals, and curiosity through code. The live link for this project points to Play Now, which gives extra context beyond the portfolio summary.

projects.title: No-Drill Curtain Hook
projects.shortSubtitle: Renter-Friendly Compression-Fit Curtain Solution
projects.summary: In a rented apartment where drilling is not allowed, hanging curtains became a challenge. Sticky hooks kept failing. I studied the wall structure, found a tight seam between the ceiling and the wall, and modeled a two-part compression-fit hook in Fusion 360 that locks in with zero screws, zero adhesive, and zero wall damage.
projects.content:
The Problem

Rented apartments make curtain hanging deceptively hard. Drilling voids the deposit. Adhesive hooks fail under curtain weight. Command strips pull paint. After the third hook failure, I looked at the wall differently — not as a surface to attach to, but as a structure to work with.

The Insight

The seam between the ceiling panel and the wall molding is a tight, load-bearing gap that runs the entire length of the room. It can't be seen from below, but it's there — and it's exactly the right width to grip a precisely modeled hook under compression.

Design

  • Two-part sliding assembly: The top hook locks into the ceiling seam. The second part slides in horizontally and locks the first part in place — no tools, no adhesive.
  • Compression grip: The hook holds through geometric interference, not friction or glue. The tighter the fit, the more load it bears.
  • Load distribution: The contact surface was widened across the seam length to spread curtain weight and prevent point-load failure.
  • Iterative tolerance testing: Three print iterations tuned the fit — too loose slips, too tight won't install. Final version installs by hand with a firm push.

Result

The hook has been holding curtains for over a year with zero wall damage. The whole system installs and uninstalls in under 30 seconds. Cost: one spool of PLA and a few hours in Fusion 360.

Tools

  • Fusion 360 — parametric CAD, tolerance control
  • FDM 3D Printing — PLA, 40% infill, 3 perimeters

projects.title: VR Experience on Oculus Quest

projects.summary: This is a collection of my creative coding sketches built with p5.js. Each piece explores different generative or interactive ideas—from visual patterns to playful simulations. Experiments include procedural pattern generators, randomized grid-based art, and color palettes.
projects.content:
Generative art sketches using p5.js

Overview

This is a collection of my creative coding sketches built with p5.js. Each piece explores different generative or interactive ideas—from visual patterns to playful simulations. Experiments include procedural pattern generators, randomized grid-based art, and color palettes.

RoleCreative Coder
Stackp5.js / JavaScript

Focus

  • Working across creative coder so the concept and execution stayed aligned.
  • Exploring visual systems through iteration, parameter tuning, and small technical experiments rather than one fixed output.
  • Using code as a medium for motion, color, and procedural variation while keeping the work understandable to viewers.
  • Building with p5.js, JavaScript to match the needs of the project without overcomplicating the stack.

Approach

This is a collection of my creative coding sketches built with p5.js. Each piece explores different generative or interactive ideas—from visual patterns to playful simulations. Experiments include procedural pattern generators, randomized grid-based art, and color palettes. The work evolved through experimentation, with each sketch or shader acting as a small probe into motion, pattern, and rendering behavior rather than a one-off aesthetic exercise. The implementation side centered on p5.js, JavaScript, chosen for fit rather than novelty.

A supporting gallery item is included to show the build in context from another angle.

Tools and Stack

  • p5.js
  • JavaScript

Outcome

This project the project reached a completed state in the portfolio. It works as a snapshot of ongoing experimentation and a way to show taste, graphics fundamentals, and curiosity through code. The page pairs the case-study writeup with the supporting media already included in the portfolio.

projects.title: 3D Poster Design with Landmarks
projects.shortSubtitle: Blender + Google Maps Data — City Landmarks at Local Time of Day
projects.summary: A poster series featuring 3D-rendered landmarks from cities across different time zones. Real-world building data was imported from the Google Maps API into Blender in two styles—realistic and graphic—then lit based on local time of day using HDRIs and custom lighting to capture each city's unique atmosphere.
projects.content:
Concept

Every city looks different at a specific hour. A poster of New York at noon reads nothing like New York at 2am. This series pins each city to a moment — not just a place — using real 3D geometry and lighting driven by local time of day.

Data Pipeline

  • Google Maps API → Blender: Real-world building geometry exported from the Maps API and imported into Blender, preserving actual footprints, heights, and geographic positions.
  • Dual rendering styles per city: Each location was rendered twice — once with full texture detail for realism, once with simplified geometry and flat color for a graphic, poster-ready look.
  • Time-driven lighting: Local sunrise/sunset data determined HDRI selection and sun angle per scene. A city lit at golden hour uses a warm low-angle HDRI; a night scene uses a city-glow HDRI with point lights for windows.

Cities Rendered

  • New York — midday, hard overhead light
  • Cologne — late afternoon, warm European light
  • Macau — dusk, mixed neon and ambient sky
  • San Francisco — morning coastal haze

Output

Final renders exported at A1 poster resolution (594×841mm at 300 DPI). The series demonstrates how geospatial data, lighting simulation, and design intention combine into something that reads as art while being technically precise.

Tools

  • Blender — modeling, lighting, rendering
  • Google Maps API — real-world 3D data source
  • HDRI Haven — time-of-day environment maps
  • Custom sun-lamp rigs — directional fill per scene

featuredMedia.alt: 3D Poster Design with Landmarks — A1 Series

mediaGallery.alt: Blender scene setup — Google Maps data import

projects.title: 3D Modeled Teddy Bear
projects.shortSubtitle: Soft-Surface Sculpting and Character Modeling in Blender
projects.summary: An early Blender sculpting exercise: a teddy bear modeled from scratch to practice soft-surface proportions, subdivision workflows, and basic character modeling fundamentals. Built with proportional editing and subdivision modifiers, finished with a basic fabric-like shader.
projects.content:
Goal

Early in my Blender learning, I needed a project that forced organic modeling — something with no hard edges, no mechanical symmetry, and a forgiving enough form that mistakes could become character. A teddy bear was the right call.

Modeling Approach

  • Primitives + proportional editing: Started from UV spheres and cylinders, then used proportional editing to push and pull the forms into plush, rounded shapes without losing volume.
  • Subdivision Surface modifier: Applied at level 3 for the final mesh — adds smoothness without manual loop cuts everywhere.
  • Merge and bridge: Limbs and ears joined to the body using bridge edge loops to maintain clean topology across seams.

Shading

A Principled BSDF with low specular and slightly elevated roughness mimics the matte, slightly fuzzy surface of plush fabric. No textures — the effect comes entirely from shader values and the softness of the subdivided mesh.

What It Taught

Organic modeling is harder than mechanical CAD because nothing is dimensionally constrained — you're judging proportion by eye. This project built the intuition for soft-surface form that I've used in every character and prop since.

Tools

  • Blender — modeling, subdivision, shading
  • Principled BSDF — soft fabric look

A modern reinterpretation of the classic snake game, transformed into a 3D space adventure. Features smooth controls, dynamic obstacles, power-ups, and progressively challenging levels that test both reflexes and spatial awareness.

  • Iterating on form, proportion, and presentation so the final output feels intentional rather than purely technical.
  • Building with Unity, Python, Javascript, Three.Js to match the needs of the project without overcomplicating the stack.

Approach

A modern reinterpretation of the classic snake game, transformed into a 3D space adventure. Features smooth controls, dynamic obstacles, power-ups, and progressively challenging levels that test both reflexes and spatial awareness. A large part of the process was visual iteration: testing composition, material treatment, and silhouette until the output felt coherent from multiple viewpoints instead of only in a single hero shot. The implementation side centered on Unity, Python, Javascript, Three.Js, chosen for fit rather than novelty.

The project is paired with an external reference in the sidebar for people who want to see the live build, repository, or related documentation.

projects.title: Magic Wand – Cartoon Shader
projects.shortSubtitle: NPR Toon Rendering with Custom Shader in Blender
projects.summary: A stylized magical wand created in Blender and rendered using a custom cartoon/toon shader. Explores NPR (non-photorealistic rendering) techniques including outline generation, color ramps, and stylized highlights — all rendered in Eevee.
projects.content:
Overview

A stylized magic wand modeled and rendered in Blender using a fully custom non-photorealistic (NPR) toon shader. The goal was to understand how cartoon rendering works at the shader level — not by using a preset, but by building the look from scratch.

Shader Breakdown

  • Color ramp on diffuse: Instead of a smooth light-to-shadow gradient, a stepped color ramp snaps shading into 2–3 hard zones — the classic cel-shaded look.
  • Specular highlight: A second color ramp on the Fresnel output creates a sharp, stylized gloss catch with a flat white zone rather than a gradient bloom.
  • Outline (inverted hull): A duplicate mesh scaled outward with face normals flipped renders as a solid black outline around the wand — the outline thickens automatically with camera distance.
  • Emission accent: The wand tip uses an emission node at low intensity for a subtle magical glow without breaking the toon aesthetic.

Renderer

All rendering done in Eevee — the real-time renderer is ideal for NPR work because shader feedback is instant, and Eevee's rasterization pipeline handles the inverted hull outline cleanly without Cycles noise.

Tools

  • Blender — modeling, shader nodes, Eevee rendering
  • Custom Toon Shader — color ramp diffuse, stepped specular, inverted hull outline

featuredMedia.alt: Magic Wand – Cartoon Shader in Blender

projects.title: Procedural Modeling in Blender
projects.shortSubtitle: Fully Procedural 3D Generation with Geometry Nodes
projects.summary: An exploration of Blender's Geometry Nodes for fully procedural 3D generation. From buildings to abstract structures, all models were created using adjustable node systems — enabling parametric design and generative variation with zero manual modeling.
projects.content:
Overview

An exploration of Blender's Geometry Nodes system for fully procedural 3D generation. Every object in this project was built without touching the mesh directly — all geometry created, instanced, and modified through node graphs.

What Was Built

  • Procedural buildings: A node graph that reads a footprint curve and extrudes floors, adds windows by instancing on face centers, and randomizes height per instance — one graph, infinite building variations.
  • Abstract structures: Grid-based instancing with math-driven transforms (sine waves, noise fields) to create organic-looking formations from purely geometric logic.
  • Parametric controls: All key values exposed as group inputs — floor count, window density, building scale — adjustable in real time with immediate mesh updates.

Why Geometry Nodes

Traditional modeling requires re-doing work whenever a design changes. A procedural graph is non-destructive by nature — adjusting one parameter ripples through the entire model. For architecture and generative design, this is the correct workflow.

Tools

  • Blender — Geometry Nodes editor, viewport
  • Procedural Modeling — no direct mesh editing

featuredMedia.alt: Procedural Modeling in Blender – Geometry Nodes

projects.title: Chess Scene – VR Modeling
projects.shortSubtitle: Blender Scene Refined in a VR Environment on Oculus Quest
projects.summary: An immersive chess room scene modeled in Blender and refined within a VR environment using Oculus Quest. The project emphasizes real-world scale, immersive design flow, and modeling ergonomics in spatial context — a fundamentally different design process than working on a flat screen.
projects.content:
Overview

A full chess room scene built in Blender and reviewed inside a VR environment on Oculus Quest. The project was part of a spatial design course focused on how VR changes the design process — not just as a viewing tool, but as a feedback loop for scale and proportion.

The VR Design Loop

  • Model in Blender: Initial scene layout, chess pieces, room geometry, and lighting built on desktop.
  • Export to VR: Scene exported and loaded into an Oculus Quest workspace for immersive review.
  • Iterate from inside: In VR, proportions that looked correct on a monitor revealed themselves to be off — a chair too large, a ceiling too low. Changes were noted and applied back in Blender.
  • Repeat: Two full review cycles before the scene felt correctly scaled for a human body inside it.

What VR Revealed

Screen-based 3D modeling collapses depth cues. VR restores them. The most important spatial relationships — how far a table sits from a wall, whether a ceiling feels oppressive or open — are nearly impossible to judge on a flat monitor. VR made those calls obvious.

Tools

  • Blender — scene modeling, lighting, materials
  • Oculus Quest — VR review and spatial validation

featuredMedia.alt: Chess Scene – VR Modeling in Blender

projects.title: Bowling Scene with Blender
projects.shortSubtitle: Stylized Bowling Alley with Motion Blur and Glow Post-Processing
projects.summary: A stylized bowling alley created in Blender, focusing on atmosphere, material shading, and object placement. Motion blur and glow effects were added for dynamic lighting and a playful, polished final render in Eevee.
projects.content:
Overview

A stylized bowling alley rendered in Blender with an emphasis on atmosphere, material contrast, and post-processing polish. The focus was getting the feeling of a bowling alley right — the waxy lanes, the overhead fluorescent glow, the sense of motion — rather than photorealistic accuracy.

Scene Details

  • Lane materials: Glossy polyurethane finish on the wood-grain lanes with a subtle anisotropic reflection that catches overhead lights in long streaks.
  • Pin and ball shading: Pins use a ceramic-like shader with high specular. The ball uses a dark resin look with subtle subsurface scattering for depth.
  • Lighting: Bank of overhead fluorescents with slight warm color cast, supplemented by side ambient fills to keep the gutter gutters from going fully dark.

Post-Processing

  • Motion blur: Applied to the ball and scattering pins to freeze the impact moment with energy rather than static stillness.
  • Bloom: Subtle glow around the fluorescent tubes and specular hot-spots — enough to feel cinematic, not enough to blow out the whites.
  • Chromatic aberration: Slight lens fringe at corners for a shot-on-camera feel.

Tools

  • Blender — modeling, shading, scene layout
  • Eevee — renderer with real-time post-processing stack
  • Compositor — motion blur, bloom, chromatic aberration

projects.title: Door Stop – Functional 3D Design
projects.shortSubtitle: Physical-Testing-Driven Prototyping in TPU Filament
projects.summary: A 3D printed door stop modeled in Fusion 360 with physical testing as the primary design constraint. Designed to work across multiple floor types and door weights, printed in TPU flexible filament and validated for grip, compression, and wear resistance.
projects.content:
The Problem

Standard rubber door stops fail on smooth floors — they slide, especially under heavy doors. Buying a better one online means guessing about floor material, door gap height, and base thickness. Modeling one means designing exactly for the situation.

Design Decisions

  • Material — TPU over PLA: PLA is rigid and slides on tile and hardwood. TPU is flexible and naturally grippy. The sole of the door stop compresses slightly under door weight, increasing contact area and friction with zero slipping.
  • Wedge geometry: Modeled at 12° taper angle — shallow enough that door weight drives the stop into the floor rather than kicking it forward, steep enough to work across varying door gap heights.
  • Ribbed sole pattern: Lateral ribs on the bottom face increase grip surface area on both hard floors and low-pile carpet without needing adhesive or suction.
  • Filleted top edge: Rounded contact face prevents the door edge from riding up and over the stop under lateral load.

Testing

Tested across three door weights and two floor types (tile, hardwood). The TPU compression and ribbed sole held across all conditions. Final filament: Shore 95A TPU at 0.2mm layer height, 25% gyroid infill for flex without collapse.

Tools

  • Fusion 360 — parametric CAD, radius control, constraint-driven geometry
  • FDM 3D Printing — TPU filament, 0.4mm nozzle
  • STL export — sliced in PrusaSlicer

mediaGallery.alt: Door stop design and print demo
